items.q: Which verb is used to say 'I am happy today' (temporary state)?
items.why: Students treat ser and estar as identical. SER for permanent traits, identity, time, origin. ESTAR for temporary states, location, ongoing actions. 'Soy contento' would suggest 'I am a happy person', not 'I'm happy right now'.

items.q: What is the 'nosotros' form of 'hablar' (to speak)?
items.why: Students confuse nosotros (we) with ellos (they). AR verbs in present: hablo, hablas, habla, HABLAMOS, habláis, hablan. The nosotros ending is -amos, not -an.

items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the preterite?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.misconception: When the action happened a long time ago.
items.random_wrong: When you're unsure of the exact date.
items.why: Students choose tense by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: preterite = completed/single ('comí' I ate); imperfect = ongoing/habitual ('comía' I used to eat / I was eating).

items.q: What is the gender of 'el día' (day)?
items.why: Students rely on '-a = feminine'. Common exceptions: el día, el mapa, el problema, el sistema, el clima (many Greek-origin -ma words). These appear constantly so must be memorised.

items.q: What does 'embarazada' mean?
items.why: A notorious faux ami that causes real confusion. 'Estoy embarazada' = 'I am pregnant'. For 'embarrassed' use 'avergonzado/a'. Native speakers laugh at this one - learners avoid by association with shame.

items.q: What does 'estar constipado' mean?
items.why: Another classic faux ami. Spanish 'constipado' refers to nasal congestion (a cold). The medical 'constipated' is 'estreñido'. Confusion can lead to awkward medical conversations.

items.q: How is 'll' typically pronounced in Spanish?
items.why: Students pronounce 'll' as English double L. In most Spanish dialects, ll is a YOD sound (like English y) or a 'zh' sound (Argentina). Never pronounced as a doubled English L.

items.q: How is the 'h' in 'hola' pronounced?
items.why: Students apply English pronunciation. Spanish H is ALWAYS silent. 'Hola' is 'OH-lah', not 'HO-lah'. (The breathy sound students associate with H comes from j or g+e/i in Spanish.)

items.q: Where do object pronouns go in 'I see him' (lo veo)?
items.why: Students apply English word order. Spanish places object pronouns BEFORE conjugated verbs. They can attach to infinitives, gerunds, and positive commands - but not after a conjugated verb in normal sentences.

items.q: Which mood follows 'quiero que' (I want that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after que. Expressions of wish, emotion, doubt, or impersonal expressions trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: 'Quiero que VENGAS' (not 'que vienes'). Different subjects require que + subjunctive.

items.q: What goes between 'veo' and 'mi amigo' in 'I see my friend'?
items.why: Students omit the personal a. When the direct object is a SPECIFIC PERSON (or pet), Spanish inserts 'a' before it. Omitting it sounds odd. 'Veo a Juan' (not 'Veo Juan').

items.q: Why does 'Me gusta el chocolate' literally translate as 'Chocolate is pleasing to me'?
items.correct: Gustar works backwards - the thing liked is the subject, the person is the indirect object.
items.misconception: Gustar means 'to like' and works exactly like English.
items.random_wrong: It is a typo for 'Yo gusto'.
items.why: Students translate gustar as 'to like' with English structure. Actually gustar = 'to be pleasing to'. So 'me gustan los libros' (books are pleasing to me) - the verb agrees with the thing, not the liker.

items.q: Why does 'inglés' have a written accent?
items.correct: It stresses the final syllable while ending in -s, which is unusual.
items.misconception: All Spanish words have accent marks.
items.why: Students treat accents as random. They follow STRESS RULES: words stressed normally don't need accents; the accent shows when stress falls outside the default position (or distinguishes homophones).

items.q: Which preposition expresses purpose ('in order to')?
items.why: Students treat por and para as identical 'for'. PARA = destination, purpose, recipient, deadline. POR = reason/cause, exchange, duration, motion through. The distinction is regularly tested.

items.q: Why does 'qué' have an accent in '¿Qué quieres?' but not always elsewhere?
items.correct: The accent distinguishes interrogative/exclamative 'qué' from relative 'que'.
items.why: Students see two spellings of the 'same word'. Spanish uses written accents to distinguish homophones with different functions: qué (question/exclamation) vs que (that/which - relative).

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' (in general) in Spanish?
items.why: English drops the article for general likes; Spanish keeps it. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: me gusta EL chocolate, me gustan LOS libros.

items.q: How do you say 'I don't see anything' in Spanish?
items.correct: No veo nada (double negative is correct).
items.why: Students avoid 'double negatives' as in English. Spanish REQUIRES them when nada/nadie/nunca follows the verb: 'no...nada', 'no...nadie', 'no...nunca'. Only one negative if it precedes ('Nada veo').

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to eat' in Spanish (near future)?
items.why: Students forget the 'a'. The structure is IR (conjugated) + A + INFINITIVE. The 'a' is essential and links the conjugated ir to the action. Voy comer is ungrammatical.

items.q: How do you say 'I wash myself' in Spanish?
items.why: Students translate word-for-word. Spanish reflexive verbs use SPECIAL PRONOUNS before the verb: me, te, se, nos, os, se. The reflexive pronoun does the work English does with 'myself/yourself'.

items.q: Where do most descriptive adjectives go relative to the noun?
items.misconception: Before the noun, as in English.
items.random_wrong: Either position with the same meaning.
items.why: Students apply English order. In Spanish, descriptive adjectives typically FOLLOW the noun. Some adjectives precede (limiting adjectives like buen, gran) and can change meaning - gran amigo (great friend) vs amigo grande (big friend).

items.q: Which preposition expresses 'for' as duration (e.g., 'for two hours')?
items.why: Students choose para because it's the default 'for'. POR is used for duration/extent of time. Para 'for two hours' would suggest a deadline ('to be ready BY two hours' time').

items.q: Which Spanish 'you' do you use with a stranger or elderly person?
items.why: Students use tú with everyone like English 'you'. Spanish distinguishes FORMAL (usted) for politeness/distance and INFORMAL (tú) for friends/family. Usted takes third-person verb forms, which trips students up.

items.q: What is the difference between 'tu' and 'tú'?
items.misconception: They are identical with optional accent.
items.random_wrong: Tú is informal, tu is formal.
items.why: Students think accents are decorative. The accent distinguishes meaning: tu hermano (your brother) vs tú eres (you are). Similar pairs: si (if) vs sí (yes); el (the) vs él (he); mi (my) vs mí (me).

items.q: How do you say 'My sister is embarrassed' in Spanish?
items.correct: Mi hermana tiene vergüenza / está avergonzada.
items.why: Notorious mistake when discussing family/relationships. 'Embarazada' = PREGNANT, not embarrassed. To say embarrassed: tener vergüenza or estar avergonzado/a. Saying your sister is pregnant when you mean embarrassed causes laughter.

items.q: In Spain, how do you address a group of friends informally?
items.why: Students don't distinguish vosotros (informal plural, Spain) from ustedes (formal plural in Spain, but used for all plural in Latin America). In Spain: tú/vosotros = informal; usted/ustedes = formal. Latin America: only ustedes for plural.

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to the beach' (talking to someone elsewhere) in Spanish?
items.why: Students confuse ir (to go) and venir (to come). IR = movement AWAY from speaker; VENIR = movement TOWARDS speaker. 'I'm going there' = voy; 'I'm coming here' = vengo. Critical for holidays/travel vocab.

items.q: How do you say 'I study English at school' in Spanish?
items.misconception: Estudio el inglés a la escuela.
items.why: Students add unnecessary articles or wrong prepositions. Languages as school subjects DROP the article: 'estudio inglés', 'me gusta el inglés' (general likes need article). Location: EN (not a) for places: en el colegio, en casa, en clase.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Spain' in Spanish?
items.why: Students drop the 'a' or use a conjugated verb. The near future is IR (conjugated) + A + INFINITIVE. The 'a' is obligatory: voy A comer, vamos A ver, vas A estudiar. Heavily tested for holiday/future plans.

items.q: How do you say 'I love films' in Spanish?
items.why: Students translate 'amar' (which is for people/love) or forget gustar/encantar structure. ENCANTAR works like gustar - backwards. Subject = películas (plural), so verb = ENCANTAN. 'Me encanta el cine' (singular subject) vs 'me encantan las películas' (plural).

items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

items.q: How do you write 'Christmas' and 'Easter' in Spanish?
items.correct: Navidad y Pascua (note: Semana Santa for Holy Week traditions).
items.why: Students add unnecessary accents or use English. Navidad has NO accent (stress on the final syllable but ends in -d which is naturally stressed). Pascua follows normal stress rules. Customs vocab: Reyes Magos (Three Kings), feria (fair).

items.q: Which mood follows 'Espero que' (I hope that)?
items.why: Students use indicative for what feels certain. Expressions of hope, wish, doubt, emotion + QUE trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: Espero que..., Quiero que..., Dudo que..., Es importante que... Common in future plans/aspirations writing.

items.q: How do you describe weather during a past holiday: 'It was sunny'?
items.why: Weather backdrops in past narratives use IMPERFECT. Preterite implies a single bounded event. 'Cuando llegué, hacía sol' = ongoing weather; llegué = completed action. Standard pattern for holiday writing.

items.q: What does 'su hermano' mean?
items.correct: Could be his/her/your(formal)/their brother - context determines which.
items.why: Students assume su = his. SU is one of Spanish's most context-dependent words. Means his, her, your (usted/ustedes), or their. Speakers often clarify with 'de él', 'de ella', 'de usted' if ambiguous: 'el hermano de él'.

items.q: How do you say 'I used the computer today'? (Spain Spanish)
items.why: In Spain, HOY (today) and recent time markers (esta semana, este mes) trigger PRETÉRITO PERFECTO (he + participle). Latin America uses preterite for these. Common error in essays: 'esta mañana fui' instead of 'esta mañana he ido'.

items.q: How do you say 'I travel by train' in Spanish?
items.why: Students use por or con. Transport always uses EN: en coche, en avión, en autobús, en tren, en barco. Exception: a pie (on foot), a caballo (on horseback). Critical holiday/travel vocab.

items.q: How do you say 'I am watching TV right now' emphasising the action is ongoing?
items.correct: Estoy viendo la televisión ahora mismo.
items.why: Students use simple present. For ONGOING actions in progress, use ESTAR + GERUNDIO (-ando/-iendo): estoy comiendo, estoy estudiando, estoy hablando. Note: estar, not ser. Useful for describing current activities in tech/free time topics.
